source: Agent-researched Blendy workflow/troubleshooting expansion, deduped against the original 53-card library.

cards.title: Hidden UV Seams for Product Textures
examples: Where do I put seams so the label does not show a cut?
My logo stretches around the corner.
cards.summary: Use hidden/real-world product edges for UV seam placement.
cards.pain: The user may keep moving UV islands randomly or accept visible texture cuts.
cards.move: Place UV seams where a real product would hide cuts: back side, underside, inside rim, or hard corner.
cards.use: Packaging, bottles, boxes, labels, and product graphics.
cards.not_use: Flat color or purely procedural materials where UV seams do not matter.
cards.diagnosis: Check UV map, seam placement, unwrap result, then material texture coordinates.
cards.say: UV seams are planned cut lines for a flat texture, so hide the cut where the viewer will not see it.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to repaint the texture before checking seams and unwrap.

cards.title: Checker Test Before Final Texture
examples: The checker squares are long rectangles.
My label gets skinny on one side.
cards.summary: Use a checker/grid material before final artwork so stretching is obvious early.
cards.pain: The user may keep adjusting final artwork while the UV map is distorted.
cards.move: Apply a checker texture, fix UV stretching until the grid reads evenly, then swap to the real artwork.
cards.use: Logos, labels, decals, fabric patterns, and repeated textures.
cards.not_use: Simple solid-color materials.
cards.diagnosis: Check scale, active UV map, checker shape, then material coordinate input.
cards.say: A checker test tells us whether the UVs are clean before the real texture distracts us.
cards.avoid: Do not diagnose lighting before checking texture coordinates.

cards.title: Average Island Scale, Then Pack
examples: Why is one panel blurry?
The texture size differs on each part.
cards.summary: Normalize UV island scale before packing so texture detail is more consistent.
cards.pain: The user may manually resize every UV island by eye.
cards.move: Use Average Island Scale, then Pack Islands with margin.
cards.use: After unwrap when repeated panels need similar texture density.
cards.not_use: Intentional hero logos or trim sheets where islands should stay oversized.
cards.diagnosis: Check island scale, intentional exceptions, pack margin, then final texture preview.
cards.say: Average island scale prevents one face from getting sharp detail while another gets blurry detail.
cards.avoid: Do not force every island equal when the design needs a larger logo island.

cards.title: Flat Label or Logo as an Image Plane
examples: I just need a logo on the front.
Can I add this sticker without UV mapping the whole thing?
cards.summary: For a flat product graphic, a separate image plane can be faster than rebuilding the material.
cards.pain: The user may overcomplicate UV mapping for a simple flat mockup.
cards.move: Import the artwork as a plane, place it just above the surface, and only UV-map if it must become part of the main material.
cards.use: Flat labels, stickers, screens, reference graphics, and quick product mockups.
cards.not_use: Curved labels, game/export assets where separate planes cause issues, or z-fighting surfaces.
cards.diagnosis: Check plane position, aspect ratio, alpha, and camera angle.
cards.say: For a flat logo, use the image like a thin sticker first.
cards.avoid: Do not recommend carving the logo into geometry unless USER needs real physical depth.

sources.title: Blender Manual: Import Images as Planes

cards.title: Simple PBR Texture Stack
examples: Where do roughness and normal maps go?
My material pack looks wrong.
cards.summary: Wire common PBR texture maps into Principled BSDF with correct data/color handling.
cards.pain: The user may plug every map into color sockets or leave normal maps unconverted.
cards.move: Base color to Base Color, roughness/metallic as Non-Color data, normal through a Normal Map node.
cards.use: Downloaded material packs and product-material setup.
cards.not_use: Procedural-only or stylized non-PBR materials.
cards.diagnosis: Identify map names, check color space, wire Principled BSDF, then compare preview/render.
cards.say: Roughness, metallic, and normal maps are data maps, not regular color images.
cards.avoid: Do not fix a bad PBR setup by changing lights first.

cards.title: Mapping Node for Texture Scale and Rotation
examples: How do I rotate the texture?
The pattern scale is wrong.
cards.summary: Adjust pattern position, scale, and rotation in shader mapping instead of resizing the mesh.
cards.pain: The user may scale the object just to change a texture pattern.
cards.move: Add Texture Coordinate plus Mapping nodes and adjust texture position, scale, and rotation there.
cards.use: Wood grain, noise, repeated patterns, and non-logo texture placement.
cards.not_use: Exact logo placement, where UV editing is usually clearer.
cards.diagnosis: Check coordinate source, mapping values, object scale, then UV map.
cards.say: Change the texture mapping, not the product dimensions.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to resize the object when only the pattern is wrong.

cards.title: Material Slots for Real Product Zones
examples: Only these faces should be metal.
Make the grip rubber.
cards.summary: Assign material slots to selected faces for hard product zones.
cards.pain: The user may split the mesh too early or assign a material to the whole object.
cards.move: Go to Edit Mode, select faces, choose material slot, click Assign, and inspect in Material Preview.
cards.use: Rubber grips, metal buttons, screens, trim, and panels.
cards.not_use: Soft gradients, dirt masks, or painted transitions.
cards.diagnosis: Check mode, selected faces, material slot, assignment, and viewport shading.
cards.say: For a hard product zone, assign the material to faces instead of splitting the object first.
cards.avoid: Do not duplicate the whole object for a small material region.

cards.title: Reference Planes in a Locked Collection
examples: I want to model from this photo.
Keep the front and side views visible.
cards.summary: Put reference images in a locked, non-rendering collection and scale them from a known measurement.
cards.pain: The user may accidentally select, move, render, or deform reference images.
cards.move: Import image planes, collect them in REFERENCES, scale them, lock selection, and disable render visibility.
cards.use: Product front/side views, characters, and model sheets.
cards.not_use: Blindly tracing perspective photos as exact orthographic drawings.
cards.diagnosis: Check reference type, scale, collection lock, and render visibility.
cards.say: A locked reference collection keeps the guide visible but out of your way.
cards.avoid: Do not trace a perspective photo as exact dimensions.

cards.title: Asset Browser for Reusable Product Parts
examples: Can I reuse this screw in other files?
I keep remaking the same knob.
cards.summary: Mark stable reusable objects/materials/node groups as assets and catalog them.
cards.pain: The user may duplicate old files manually and lose source organization.
cards.move: Finish a clean reusable part, mark it as asset, assign catalog/preview, then drag from Asset Browser later.
cards.use: Screws, knobs, buttons, materials, and lighting rigs.
cards.not_use: Unstable work-in-progress objects.
cards.diagnosis: Check asset library path, mark-as-asset state, catalog, and link/append expectations.
cards.say: Reusable product bits belong in the Asset Browser once they are stable.
cards.avoid: Do not make every experimental object an asset.

cards.title: Pack External Textures Before Sending a Blend File
examples: Textures disappeared on another computer.
How do I send the blend file with textures?
cards.summary: Pack resources or use relative paths before moving/sharing a textured project.
cards.pain: The user may send only the .blend while textures remain in a local folder.
cards.move: Use File > External Data to pack resources or make paths relative, then reopen/test from the new location.
cards.use: Sharing, archiving, moving, or handing off textured scenes.
cards.not_use: Very large texture/video libraries where a clean linked folder is intentional.
cards.diagnosis: Check image paths, pack/relative setting, and test on a copied folder.
cards.say: A .blend file may not contain its image textures unless you pack them.
cards.avoid: Do not rebuild materials before checking external file paths.

cards.title: Geometry Nodes Scatter for Rivets, Screws, and Dots
examples: Do I need to place every screw by hand?
I need hundreds of repeated bumps.
cards.summary: Use Geometry Nodes instances for large repeated surface details.
cards.pain: The user may duplicate and place every screw/dot manually.
cards.move: Distribute points on the surface and instance one detail object; realize only when export needs real mesh.
cards.use: Rivets, pores, dots, buttons, and repeated surface decoration.
cards.not_use: Exact hand-placed product details or simple straight rows where Array is easier.
cards.diagnosis: Check instance count, target surface, exact placement needs, and export needs.
cards.say: For hundreds of repeated details, let Blender instance them instead of placing each one.
cards.avoid: Do not recommend Geometry Nodes for three simple duplicates.

cards.title: Numeric Transform Instead of Eyeballing
examples: Move exactly 2 cm.
How do I rotate it 90 degrees?
cards.summary: Use axis-locked numeric transforms for exact product placement.
cards.pain: The user may drag parts until they look close and fight alignment later.
cards.move: Use transforms like G X value, R Z 90, or typed scale values.
cards.use: Measured product offsets, rotations, and alignment.
cards.not_use: Organic sculpting or snapping-to-surface tasks.
cards.diagnosis: Check units, axis/orientation, transform values, and whether snapping would be better.
cards.say: For product work, typed transforms beat eyeballing.
cards.avoid: Do not ask USER to manually nudge exact mechanical offsets.

cards.title: Orthographic Camera for Product Spec Shots
examples: I need a straight-on front render.
How do I show dimensions without perspective?
cards.summary: Use an orthographic camera for clean product/spec-sheet views.
cards.pain: The user may fight perspective distortion by moving the camera around.
cards.move: Switch camera to Orthographic, align to front/side/top, adjust Orthographic Scale, then render.
cards.use: Product sheets, dimension views, turnarounds, and straight-on reference renders.
cards.not_use: Dramatic hero renders where perspective sells depth.
cards.diagnosis: Check camera type, alignment, orthographic scale, and render region.
cards.say: For spec shots, orthographic removes perspective distortion.
cards.avoid: Do not keep tweaking perspective focal length for a flat spec render.

cards.title: UV Texture Stretches or Smears After Unwrap
examples: My image is stretched.
The checker squares are long rectangles.
cards.summary: A stretched texture is usually a UV, scale, or coordinate problem.
cards.pain: The user may repaint the texture instead of diagnosing UVs.
cards.move: Apply/check scale, inspect active UV map, add/check seams, unwrap again, and use a checker to verify.
cards.use: Visible texture/checker/logo distortion on a mesh.
cards.not_use: Flat solid materials with no texture coordinates involved.
cards.diagnosis: Check scale, active UV map, seams, unwrap, UV island proportions, then shader coordinates.
cards.say: A stretched texture is usually a UV/scale/coordinate problem, not a lighting problem.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to repaint the texture first.

cards.title: Texture Uses Wrong UV Map or Coordinate Space
examples: I unwrapped it but the texture ignores it.
Generated coordinates look different than UV.
cards.summary: If the shader is not using UV coordinates, a good unwrap can still look wrong.
cards.pain: The user may keep unwrapping while the shader reads a different coordinate space.
cards.move: Check active/render UV map, Texture Coordinate output, UV Map node name, Mapping node, and object scale.
cards.use: Texture does not follow the UV layout or changes with object scale.
cards.not_use: Intentionally procedural materials that do not need UVs.
cards.diagnosis: Check coordinate source, UV map name, active/render UV, Mapping node, then scale.
cards.say: The unwrap can be perfect and still ignored if the shader is using Generated or Object coordinates.
cards.avoid: Do not assume the unwrap failed before checking the shader vector input.

cards.title: Textures Turn Pink or Missing After Moving the Blend File
examples: Everything is pink.
It worked until I moved the folder.
cards.summary: Pink usually means Blender cannot find the image file.
cards.pain: The user may rebuild materials while image files are simply missing.
cards.move: Use Find Missing Files, pack resources if sharing, and convert to relative paths.
cards.use: Materials turn pink or textures vanish after moving/sending a file.
cards.not_use: Materials with no image texture files.
cards.diagnosis: Check image nodes, file paths, missing-file reports, pack/relative path settings.
cards.say: Pink usually means the image path is broken, not that the material logic is wrong.
cards.avoid: Do not rebuild the shader before checking paths.

cards.title: Normal, Roughness, or Metallic Map Looks Wrong From Color Space
examples: My normal map looks broken.
Roughness looks washed out.
cards.summary: PBR data maps need Non-Color handling and correct node routing.
cards.pain: The user may change lighting while data maps are interpreted as colors.
cards.move: Set roughness/metallic/normal maps as Non-Color data and route normals through a Normal Map node.
cards.use: PBR material packs and normal/roughness/metallic map confusion.
cards.not_use: Plain color-only materials.
cards.diagnosis: Check map type, color space, node route, strength, and UV.
cards.say: Roughness, metallic, and normal maps are data, not visible color.
cards.avoid: Do not fix this with lighting tweaks first.

cards.title: Alpha Texture Is Still Opaque or Renders Wrong
examples: PNG transparency is black.
Alpha is plugged in but the object is solid.
cards.summary: Transparency needs both alpha data and material/render settings.
cards.pain: The user may cut geometry manually before testing alpha settings.
cards.move: Verify image alpha, connect alpha to Principled Alpha, and check engine/material transparency settings.
cards.use: PNG decals, leaves/cards, glass cutouts, and transparent planes.
cards.not_use: Actual holes that need geometry thickness.
cards.diagnosis: Check image alpha, node wiring, material settings, engine, and shadow mode.
cards.say: The alpha channel needs to be wired and the material must allow transparency.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to delete the plane before testing alpha.

cards.title: Exported Model Is White or Missing Textures
examples: It looks fine in Blender but white in Unity.
GLB has no textures.
cards.summary: Export formats have material limits; simple PBR travels best.
cards.pain: The user may assume every Blender shader node exports perfectly.
cards.move: Use Principled-compatible materials, bake procedural textures if needed, and include/copy images.
cards.use: GLB/FBX/engine/viewer output is white or missing textures.
cards.not_use: Purely Blender-internal render issues.
cards.diagnosis: Check format, shader simplicity, image paths, pack/copy settings, and target app limitations.
cards.say: The export may be fine structurally while the target app cannot read Blender-only shader nodes.
cards.avoid: Do not rebuild geometry before checking material/export compatibility.

cards.title: Render Engine Changes Reflections, Shadows, Glass, or Materials
examples: Cycles looks different than Eevee.
Workbench render has no real materials.
cards.summary: Eevee, Cycles, and Workbench do not show materials/features the same way.
cards.pain: The user may tweak materials without noticing the engine changed.
cards.move: Check render engine, viewport shading, engine-specific material settings, lights/world, and color management.
cards.use: Engine differences, glass, reflections, shadows, and material preview/render mismatches.
cards.not_use: Simple camera crop/framing issues.
cards.diagnosis: Check engine, viewport mode, material settings, lights/world, and color management.
cards.say: Render engines are not interchangeable preview modes.
cards.avoid: Do not treat every render mismatch as a bad material.

cards.title: Object Is Cut Off by Camera or Viewport Clipping
examples: Half my model disappears when I zoom.
The render cuts through the object.
cards.summary: Clipping can hide geometry without deleting it.
cards.pain: The user may think the mesh is broken or deleted.
cards.move: Determine viewport vs camera render, then check camera clip start/end, viewport clip start/end, and scene scale.
cards.use: Objects disappear near/far, render slices geometry, or zooming clips the model.
cards.not_use: Hidden collections or disabled render visibility.
cards.diagnosis: Check viewport/camera clipping, scene scale, and camera position.
cards.say: This can be a view/camera clipping range problem, not a destroyed mesh.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to rebuild the mesh before checking clipping.

cards.title: Render or Clipping Region Was Left On
examples: Only a rectangle renders.
I can only see a box-shaped slice.
cards.summary: A leftover render/clipping region can make the scene look broken.
cards.pain: The user may resize the camera or move objects when a region toggle is active.
cards.move: Check render border/crop, clear render region, check viewport clipping region, then camera framing.
cards.use: Only a rectangle renders or the viewport is boxed/sliced.
cards.not_use: The object is simply outside the camera frame with no active region.
cards.diagnosis: Check render region/crop, viewport clipping region, and camera framing.
cards.say: Before moving the camera, clear the render or clipping region.
cards.avoid: Do not change model scale or camera until region state is checked.

cards.title: Imported or Exported Model Is Tiny, Huge, Sideways, or Backwards
examples: It imports sideways.
The model faces the wrong direction.
cards.summary: Import/export axis and unit settings often beat manually rotating/scaling mesh data.
cards.pain: The user may rotate/scale mesh data blindly instead of using format settings.
cards.move: Check file format, unit scale, object transforms, forward/up axis settings, and apply transform only when appropriate.
cards.use: FBX/OBJ/glTF axis and scale handoff issues.
cards.not_use: Purely internal Blender modeling scale questions.
cards.diagnosis: Check format, units, transforms, forward/up axis, and target app.
cards.say: Axis/export settings often solve this better than manually rotating the mesh.
cards.avoid: Do not blindly rotate the mesh before checking exporter settings.

cards.title: Export Missing Objects Because Include Settings Filtered Them Out
examples: Only one object exported.
The GLB is missing parts.
cards.summary: Missing export parts often come from Include filters, not broken geometry.
cards.pain: The user may duplicate or merge objects before checking exporter filters.
cards.move: Check export Include settings, selection state, object/collection visibility, active view layer, and object types.
cards.use: GLB/FBX/OBJ exports omit visible or expected objects.
cards.not_use: Materials/textures are missing but objects export.
cards.diagnosis: Check include filters, selection, visibility, view layer, and object types.
cards.say: Before changing the model, check whether the exporter was told to export only selected/visible/renderable objects.
cards.avoid: Do not merge objects until exporter filters are checked.

cards.title: Geometry Nodes Modifier Shows Nothing Because Output Chain Is Broken
examples: Geometry Nodes made my object disappear.
Nothing happens after adding nodes.
cards.summary: If the Group Output has no geometry path, the modifier has nothing to show.
cards.pain: The user may rebuild the node tree before checking the final output socket.
cards.move: Check Group Input to Group Output geometry path, socket types, muted nodes, modifier enabled, and object type.
cards.use: Geometry Nodes no-output or disappearing-object failures.
cards.not_use: Normal mesh modifiers like Bevel unless Geometry Nodes is involved.
cards.diagnosis: Check modifier visibility, Group Output geometry input, socket types, muted nodes, and object type.
cards.say: Trace the geometry line into Group Output first.
cards.avoid: Do not rebuild every node before checking the output socket.

cards.title: Geometry Nodes Instances Cannot Be Edited Like Real Mesh
examples: I can see the copies but cannot select them.
The instances will not get individual materials.
cards.summary: Instances are lightweight references until they are realized/applied.
cards.pain: The user may expect generated instances to have normal Edit Mode vertices.
cards.move: Explain instance vs real geometry, check Realize Instances placement, and warn about density/export impact.
cards.use: Generated objects cannot be individually edited or materialized.
cards.not_use: Ordinary duplicated mesh objects.
cards.diagnosis: Check instance nodes, Realize Instances placement, material assignment, and export needs.
cards.say: Instances are not normal editable mesh until you realize/apply them.
cards.avoid: Do not tell USER to apply without explaining it makes generated geometry real.

cards.title: Edit Mode Vertices, Edges, or Faces Are Invisible
examples: I am in Edit Mode but cannot see vertices.
The mesh is there but no edges show.
cards.summary: The mesh can exist while overlays or hidden elements make edit controls invisible.
cards.pain: The user may think the mesh was deleted.
cards.move: Check active object, mode, viewport overlay toggle, selection mode, hidden geometry, and X-Ray/wire display.
cards.use: Mesh edit controls are missing or hidden.
cards.not_use: The entire object is hidden by collection/local view.
cards.diagnosis: Check active object, mode, overlay toggle, selection mode, hidden geometry, and X-Ray/wire.
cards.say: First check overlays and hidden geometry before assuming the mesh is gone.
cards.avoid: Do not assume deletion before checking overlays and hidden geometry.

cards.title: Object Hidden by Collection, View Layer, Local View, or Object Type Filter
examples: The object is gone but still in the outliner.
Only one object is visible now.
cards.summary: Visibility layers can hide objects without deleting them.
cards.pain: The user may append/reimport or rebuild an asset unnecessarily.
cards.move: Check Local View, object hide flags, collection restriction toggles, view-layer exclude, object type visibility, and render visibility.
cards.use: Objects vanish, only one object is visible, or viewport/render visibility differs.
cards.not_use: Edit-mode hidden vertices only.
cards.diagnosis: Check Local View, object hide flags, collection restriction toggles, view layer, object type visibility, render visibility.
cards.say: The object may still be there; the view layer or collection might be hiding it.
cards.avoid: Do not reimport the object before checking visibility toggles.
